text: So this is a lady who has a sarcoma.
She is undergoing a lobectomy of the right lower lobe.
So the patient is positioned in a lateral decudus position and the rib spaces are identified.
This is the course of the 10th rib.
We're going to go in over the 9th rib on this particular lady.
You really want that anterior port site, which is the port that you're gonna staple through to be as anterior as possible and in a rib space that's wide enough.
And then the other ports are gonna be placed with the camera port, usually in the mid axillary line, and then other ports approximately at least a hand's breadth away from each other.
We're going to inject liposomal epivacaine, and then the CAM report is placed in first.
Typically, I'll use a hemostat to break the pleural seal, allow the lung to drop down before putting the trocar in.
And then once the trocar goes in,
then I always place a camera in the chest first prior to commencing carbon dioxide insufflation just to make sure that you're not insufflating into the lung.
So at this point, warm carbon dioxide is instilled having confirmed an intrapleural location of the cannula.
But now what we're going to do is we're going to do a posterior intercostal nerve block over about five interspaces using liposomal bupivacaine.
This is a non-FDA approved indication for this drug, by the way.
So typically I'll dilute 266 milligrams of liposomal bupivacaine to a final volume of 40 cc's and then give
20 cc's over 5 interspaces, so about 4 cc's per interspace.
If you inject the drug just at the posterior post border of the innermost intercostal, you get this nice posterior spread usually that tracks back to the paravertebral space.
So once the block is in, then we will place our other trocars.
And all of these trocars are being placed in the eighth intercostal space over the top of the ninth rib.
So you don't need a hemostat really for these.
Just a skin incision.
I like to use the sharp trocar, but the blunt one works OK as well.
We want to position these trochars so that the fulcrum point, which is marked by the thicker black line, rests within the intercostal tissue.
So that's the posterior one.
Then our left-handed port is placed.

Now what we're going to do is we're going to put the camera through the posterior port because when you make that anterior port, because we're so low down, it's beneficial to see where the cannula is coming in because it is possible to place it in a sub-diaphragmatic position and certainly almost inevitably go sub-diaphragmatic unless you use carbon dioxide in my experience.
So this is a 12 millimeter port and this is the port that we're going to be using our right-handed instrument, but also our stapling devices.
So we're using an XI on this patient.
And the nice thing about the XI is that you can approach the patient really from any angle you want.
The gantry moves independent of the machine.
And so you can,
really position the gantry from either side of the table.
So all of these trocars are in a line parallel to the 9th rib and so we rotate the gantry so that hit 2 is parallel to that 9th rib.
The camera port is connected first, camera is placed inside.
There is a tracking option, an automated tracking option with the XI.
I have not found this beneficial.
So at this point, the other arms are connected to the trocars.
It's a good idea to try and kind of get the general orientation of the arms similar to the orientation of the trocars.
What you'll see is once I'm connecting them, then I'm going to push the arm clearance button to lower the arm of the trocar down just like that.
Sorry, the arm of the robot down.
That arm clearance button, by bringing all of the arms down, helps prevent clashes.
Now, unlike the SI, where you want to spread those robotic arms as far apart as possible, with the XI, you actually want everything quite close together.
There's an inner stylet adapter for the 12mm port that allows use of 7mm instruments.
So we watch the instruments coming in one by one, and I generally park the tips up near the apex of the chest.
Here we're just pulling back that posterior trochar a little bit because it was a little bit too far in.
And once all those instruments are in the chest, then
both the resident and I are going to go over to the consoles to do the case.
So I don't have a first assist at the bedside except for specimen extraction, typically.
Or if there's bleeding, occasionally you need to have somebody do suction for you.
But as you'll see, you can often use just a flexible catheter to suction from the console.
So we begin
by retracting the lung in a cephalad fashion, exposing the inferior pulmonary ligament.
We're going to take this down.
We'll take out some inferior pulmonary ligament nodes and parasophageal nodes as well because they're easy to do, although for a sarcoma you could argue that you don't need to do this.
Just divide the ligament.
but certainly for lung cancer cases, we would routinely take the nose from the inferior ligament first and then the subcarinal space.
So I like to use a bipolar dissector.
Usually I'll start off with a setting of about three on the bipolar, but sometimes I have to go a little higher
And it's important when you use the bipolar that you realize it's a combination of burning and then pulling apart the burnt tissue.
But it's really important that you apply that electricity as the jaws of the bipolar instrument close because if you don't have a tiny gap, you don't get as good cauterization of tissue.
So I'm applying the electricity as the jaws of the instrument are closing on the tissue, and then making sure that there has been a clottery of the tissue before you pull it apart.

You gotta be careful in the inferior pulmonary ligament, as you know, because there are small vessels that can sometimes retract and oftentimes they'll not be bleeding initially, but then may open up in the recovery room or something.
So I'm very careful to make sure that all of these little inferior pulmonary ligament vessels are well sealed.
So this is a Parasoft Geolar Level 8R node that we're taking down now.
I generally like to hold the perinodal tissue, not the actual node itself, because oftentimes they're fragile and will fracture and fragment if you grab the node themselves.
And if you do have to grab a node, generally it's better to grab a big bite of a node rather than a small bite of a node.
Now because I don't have a first assistant here, the Scribtech is going to be the person taking the specimens in and out of the chest.
So what you want to do is you want to line up that right-handed port, which is through the 12mm port, because he's going to pass a standard 5mm laparoscopic instrument through that port.
You want to line it up in a position where it's easy for him to pass the instrument.
Then you kind of have to feed the nodes to him.
As you take down the ligament, then you constantly have to readjust the traction.

I generally like passive retraction, where we're pushing tissue rather than grabbing tissue, especially for I have a lung tissue, but this is a lower lobe, and so if there's a break in the visceral pleura, it doesn't really matter.
It's coming out anyhow.
So another 8R lymph node.
Now, what's happening here is that the resident is doing this dissection is sort of operating off the top of the screen.
So it's important to remember to adjust your camera periodically so that you're centered.
And little spreads helps define the tissue.
It is not necessary to spread to the full extent of the instrument.
Oftentimes, there's a tendency to do that.
But especially if you're dissecting around vessels, you want to make just tiny little spreads.
Here's a 9R lymph node that we're removing.
So here we're just using passive retraction with the flexed tip-up grasper.
Another thing you can do is use some form of a sponge to help retract.
It's a nice gentle way of retracting on the lung tissue.
So we use dental rolls that have a radiopaque strip placed inside them so that we can identify them on radiographs, but you can use rolled up cigars or gauze or whatever.
So we're going to continue marching up here along the posterior pleura, taking out lymph glands as we find them.
In this area, particularly around the back of the bronchus,
very important to separate the pleura before dividing it to avoid a bronchial injury.
In this patient, we're going to take out the level 7 lymph nodes.
Again, less so for oncologic reasons, but they will facilitate the division of the
right lower lobe bronchus.

In general, in robotic surgery, robotic lung surgery anyhow, removal of the lymph glands ahead of time really facilitates identification of structures and facilitates getting around arteries, veins, bronchi.
I try to avoid grabbing the nodal packet as much as possible, although it's often unavoidable, mainly because I end up fracturing the lymph nodes oftentimes if I do that.
And oftentimes it seems no matter how diligent I am about burning these vessels that it's not uncommon to get into some bleeding.
as you'll see.
In this case, it was fairly trivial.
Sometimes you need to control it with an additional suction port or something.
So here I'm going to use the back of the bipolar instrument to provide hemostasis.
So in order to complete the fissure, we're going to have to identify the takeoff of the right upper lobe bronchus and the top border of the bronchus intermedius.

So there grabbing the sponge allows sort of an atraumatic passive retraction of the lung tissue.
So now we're dissecting up between the lower lobe bronchus and the superior aspect of the inferior pulmonary vein.
Now we'll flip the lung posteriorly and identify the anterior aspect of that lower lobe vein.
Very important, obviously, to ensure that the right middle lobe vein is not aberrant and coming off of the right lower lobe or inferior pulmonary vein.
And in this case, it does not appear to be.
So next we're going to get into the fissure.
And in this patient, it's relatively easy.
So use of the bipolar here using very, very small little spreads with the closed tips pushing on the artery.
And judicious application of electrocautery is all that's needed.
The nice thing about bipolar is that you have very little lateral thermal spread.
So it really is very safe to dissect
use the bipolar right on the vessels.

are S, superior lymph nodes here.

Now we'll dissect out the 11RI, the 11R inferior node.
And this was a little bit more stuck to the surrounding tissue than usual.
Just judicious use of the bipolar is all that's required.
So there's the middle lobe artery.
One of them, anyhow.
And now here, there seems to be this average branch of the middle lobe vein to the S4 segment of the middle lobe.
So it was very important to dissect that out to make sure that that was not coming off of the inferior pulmonary vein.
You can see where I grabbed that node and fractured it, which is a nuisance.
And oftentimes it's better to try and grab just the perinodal tissue or just sort of bluntly retract the node rather than grabbing it to avoid doing what I just did there.
So there you can see the right middle lobe bronchus inferior to the node.
So now we're going to dissect down inferior to the middle lobe bronchus and try to complete the fissure, following that vein down to its origin.

And once we see a window in there, then we can pass our stapler.
And occasionally I'll pass a vessel loop or something here, but in this instance it was pretty wide open.
We're gonna use a robotic controlled stapler, 30 millimeter.
And since this is fairly thin lung tissue, we're just gonna use a blue load cartridge.
The nice thing about this stapler is that it comes with a ski tip, which is very helpful in terms of passing it around tissue like this.
So once the instrument is locked down and clamped, it'll fire only if there is sufficient tissue compression, so-called smart clamping.
These staplers do give a very nice even cut on stapling.
So there's our area of dissection from the posterior aspect on the inferior border of the right lower lobe bronchus.
At this point, now we can start dissecting underneath the artery there.
Again, getting the lymph nodes out of the way is helpful.
Now at this point we need to complete the posterior oblique fissure.

So that's going to have to be dealt with.
So it's unclear to me at this point whether or not that's the posterior ascending artery or is it just a high takeoff of the superior segmental artery.

plural space and not the peritoneum.
The nice thing with the robot, the XI robot, is that the camera will fit in any of the ports.

I'm gonna check the bronchial stump to make sure that it is airtight.
And then a single chest tube is placed, usually a 20 French or 24 French.
I'm going to bring that chest tube out through the anterior incision.
Quarts are removed under direct vision to make sure that there's no intercostal artery injury.
